g.text: Implementation of Stacks and Queues using Linked Lists

g.data-narration: Array-based stacks and queues have fixed size. Linked lists solve this with dynamic memory allocation, growing and shrinking as needed.
A self-referential structure contains a pointer to another instance of itself, forming the foundation of linked list data structures.
A stack is LIFO - Last In First Out. In linked list implementation, we add and remove elements from the head for O(1) efficiency.
The push operation creates a new node, points it to current top, then updates top to the new node - three simple steps.
g.text: Stack Push Operation - Step by Step

g.data-narration: Pop operation saves top data, moves top to next node, frees old node memory, and returns saved data - all O(1).
A queue is FIFO - First In First Out. Linked list queue uses both front and rear pointers for efficient enqueue at tail, dequeue at head.
Enqueue adds to the rear. If queue empty, both front and rear point to new node. Otherwise, link old rear to new and update rear.
Dequeue removes from front. Save front data, move front to next, free old node. If front becomes NULL, set rear to NULL too.
Here's the complete code structure for both stack and queue implementations with node structures and key operations.
g.text: struct StackNode { int data; struct StackNode* next; };
struct Queue { Node* front; Node* rear; };
push() O(1) | pop() O(1) | peek() O(1) | isEmpty() O(1)
enqueue() O(1) | dequeue() O(1) | front() O(1) | rear() O(1)
No isFull() needed - queue grows until system memory exhausted!

g.data-narration: Compare array vs linked list implementations across memory usage, flexibility, performance, and implementation complexity.
Linked list implementation offers dynamic size, efficient memory usage, no fixed capacity limit, and all operations remain O(1).
Disadvantages include memory overhead for pointers, complex implementation, need for careful pointer management, and cache performance issues.
Stacks power function calls, expression evaluation, and undo features. Queues handle CPU scheduling, print queues, and BFS algorithms.
g.text: • BFS in graphs, print job scheduling

g.data-narration: Critical memory management: always free nodes when removing, check for NULL pointers, avoid memory leaks, handle edge cases.
